It keeps the blood moving in the veins, and that lowers the predisposition for the blood to clot.
So at this point, that's the most useful recommendation.
Moving your legs about every two hours is the general advice.
And researchers Fritz Rosendahl says in order to do this, people really need to be awake.
Catnaps, he says, are okay, but...
People should not get into a really deep sleep.
I think that's not a good idea.
So that means keep alcohol consumption to a minimum.
And Rosendahl advises leave the sleeping pills at home.
Allison Aubrey, NPR News.
You can find a list of what puts you at risk of getting a blood clot while flying at npr.org.
There, you'll also find answers to listener questions for an entirely different story about how mothers can strengthen their relationships with their daughters.
One of the most senior Taliban commanders is in custody.
Pakistan's security forces raided a home in the southwestern city of Quetta earlier this week.
They say they seized the man described as a former Taliban defense minister.
This is described as the first arrest of a senior Taliban figure since the U.S.-led invasion of Afghanistan in 2001.
We're going to go now to New York Times correspondent Carlotta Gall who is in Peshawar, Pakistan today.
And Carlotta, could this really be the first time in all these years that they've arrested a senior Taliban figure?
Yes, and that's why it's significant.
Because Pakistan has often been criticized for not moving against the Taliban.
They haven't seen them as a threat.
It's known that there's a lot of sympathy for the Taliban, especially in the border areas.
And so the authorities who have gone against senior al-Qaida figures have always been reluctant or have said they cannot move against the Taliban.
And he is the former defense minister.
He is a member of the top leadership council, so clearly an important figure to be arresting.
Anything noteworthy about the timing here, given that we're told that the arrest took place on Monday, which is when Vice President Dick Cheney happened to be in Pakistan?
Yes, it's hard to know if there was a specific timing.
What has been clear to us though here is that the pressure has been building on Pakistan for some months, because the Taliban insurgency in the south of Afghanistan was so strong last year.
There are fears that there will be another onslaught with a string offensive in coming months.
So I think the pressure has been building from the U.S.

and from NATO countries to try and help, not only stopping infiltration across from Pakistan, but actually, to go after the leaders who are largely believed to be enjoying a safe sanctuary in Pakistan and particularly in Quetta, this town in the south where Mullah Obaidullah was picked up.
Are there lots of Taliban figures said to be in that city near the border?
Well, Pakistani authorities deny it.
The minister of information said to me, if there are any they were very few.

But Afghan officials have been saying for years that the main leaders are there and they have their families there, which helps them fight across the border because they always have a safe sanctuary to retreat to.
I suppose this raises the question if there are said to be many Taliban figures there and they finally arrested one, if that counts as a sign of new commitment?
Yes.
I mean, I think what the long-term watchers have always noticed is that when the pressure ramps up from the U.S.
and allies, Pakistan does move and makes a big effort and then gets someone.
And then things tend to slide and go quiet again and the pressure eases.
Well, we must mention though that in December, the Pakistani intelligence did cooperate in also tracking another very senior Taliban leader.
Akhtar Mohammad Osmani, who was seen as a key financial operator - and he was also in the leadership council.
And he was tracked with the help of Pakistan when he entered Afghanistan.
He was then killed in an air strike by U.S.
forces.
So this is the second, in other words, in a couple of months.
And that is new, because, as you say, we haven't seen that since the war started and American forces moved against the Taliban regime in Afghanistan.
New York Times correspondent Carlotta Gall is in Peshawar, Pakistan.
Carlotta, good to talk with you again.
Thank you.
(Soundbite of music)
This is NPR News.
While President-elect Obama focused today on creating jobs, there are concerns about support for people who are out of work.
Last week, President Bush signed a bill that extends federal unemployment benefits for three months.
Before the unemployed can tap into federal money, they first have to exhaust their state unemployment benefits, and some states are running out of money.
We're going to hear now from Indiana where unemployment funds may run out early next month.
Here's Marianne Holland of Indiana Public Broadcasting.
Seven years ago, Indiana had $1.4 billion in its reserve to pay people finding themselves out of work.
Today there's only $40 million left.
With monthly payouts topping $66 million, a crisis is at hand.
It's happening as the need for money is going up.
At an unemployment office in downtown Indianapolis, people are lined up minutes after it opens.
Parents with small children are seated waiting to be helped, and there's a stream of people coming through the door.
When Debbie Geralds(ph) finally leaves the office, she hurries to the big silver pickup truck she's borrowed from her dad to get here.
Inside, the middle-aged woman with short salt-and-pepper hair shivers while waiting for the truck to warm up.
She says she needs this money and doesn't care how the state gets it.
I don't understand why, but I think whatever they have to do, do it.
There's a lot of people out of work.
Well, thank you, it's been wonderful.
That was former NFL star Bobby Howard.
He's an education specialist for LifeLink of Georgia, and organ donation organization.
And news organizations themselves may not have been paying enough attention.

As one of several rough measures, I reviewed more than a hundred articles published before early 2007 by the New York Times with some version of the terms collateralized debt obligation or securitization.
Those are the financial vehicles that got us in trouble.
Few of the Times's stories were cautionary; none of those stories landed on page one.
By that measure, the Wall Street Journal looks a little better.
Reporters Mark Whitehouse and Greg Ip warned readers of the risks in a front page piece in November 2005, and Whitehouse followed up with another a year later.
Even in journalism, real estate is destiny.
If a paper wants your attention, it slaps the story on the front page.
But if it really wants your attention, it covers the story relentlessly.
Where was the critical mass?
The Times's Morgenson wrote impassioned columns about each half of the equation: the disintegration of the subprime mortgage market, and also executive pay, because as she says…
Many, many banks took on far too much risk trying to juice their profits, and often one of the results of juiced profits is higher pay.
Morgenson says she began putting the two halves of the equation together in 2007.
That brings us back to Business Week.
Coy and his colleagues started reporting on the dangers of securitizing real estate investments back in 2002, but he says people just didn't want to hear it during the real estate boom.
And yet even Coy ultimately says media outlets should have sought more ways to get people to listen.
If everything we had said and written came true, then we might have expected some of this to happen.
But I think there was a failure of imagination among economists, policymakers, as well as journalists.
The nation's best news organizations didn't exactly fail to report on these financial weapons of mass destruction.
Maybe they just didn't trust their own reporting enough to hammer it home, at least not to the point where people who were reading and watching got the message.
David Folkenflik, NPR News, New York.
Many Americans out of work right now face a tough choice.
They can accept a lower paying job that is available in this tough economy or they can hold out for something closer to the paycheck they lost.
NPR's Larry Abramson reports on the choices facing many unemployed workers.

After a year of negative economic growth, a lot of workers out there are going through the various phases of unemployment from I'm going to get a job tomorrow enthusiasm to a kind of Zen patience to total despair.
Andrew Stettner of the National Employment Law Project says this recession has swept in a lot of workers who are totally new to this mind-numbing ego-bruising exercise.
Most people you talk to will say I've never had this problem before.
If you're a go-go salesman like Rick Carter(ph) who's been unemployed since August, the waiting can be hard to take.
I'll be honest with you.
Looking back at it, I'm a little surprised that something didn't happen because there was a lot of activity and there was a lot of interviews.
A few things came very, very close.
You know nothing's happened quite yet.
Carter lives with his wife and 11-year-old daughter in a spacious home in the Virginia suburbs of Washington, D.C.
He used to earn six figures selling security technology and was laid off after a takeover.
At first, he saw this as an opportunity to do even better, but after four months he's wondering whether he may have to settle for less.
I'm finding now that there's opportunities out there that when you first are presented them, you know, it may be that it could be a significantly lower, what we call, base salary.
Meaning that it would be up to Carter to earn more money through commissions.
That could be tough to do in slow times.
But Carter at 53 years old still sees himself as young and aggressive.
In addition to the spiritual downer, being unemployed during the holidays, Rick Carter says many companies just stop hiring from Thanksgiving on.
In interviews they told him they would reassess after the first of the year.
But when I spoke to him just before the holidays, he was determined not to let this drag on.
It may happen, but I want to be more aggressive than that.
I would like to be back to work sooner than that.
The recruiter who introduced me to Rick Carter is Brock Boyd.
As CEO of CMI Careers in Vienna, Virginia, he sees a lot of successful executives and marketing people who spend months refusing to think about a job with lower pay.
The trouble is that once they do, oftentimes they've now been out of work for five, eight, 10 months.
And now it becomes extremely challenging to find even the compensation that five to eight months ago would have been achievable.
Boyd says there's no magic number of months workers should wait, but he says white-collar types may be viewed as damaged goods if they're out of the workforce for too long.
Some workers say as long as their unemployment insurance keeps paying out, they don't feel the need to look at lower paying jobs.
But after a full year without work, Ken Stalma(ph) of South Plainfield, New Jersey, says he's feeling the pressure.
I used to eat at the Four Seasons in Manhattan in the '80s when money was falling out of your pockets.
Now I'm relegated to cooking meals back at home by myself.
